items.title: Genetic Predisposition in Atopic Dermatitis (AD)
items.content: Atopic dermatitis has a significant genetic component. When both parents have AD, over 80% of their children are affected. If only one parent is affected, the prevalence drops to slightly more than 50%. A common genetic defect involves mutations in the gene encoding filaggrin.
keyPoints: Genetic predisposition: >80% of children manifest AD if both parents are affected; >50% if one parent is affected.
Filaggrin mutation contributes to impaired epidermal barrier, a characteristic defect in AD.

items.title: Clinical Presentation and Course of Atopic Dermatitis (AD)
items.content: Atopic dermatitis typically presents with pruritus and a course marked by exacerbations and remissions. Lesions are typical of eczematous dermatitis, often lasting more than 6 weeks. Half of the patients present within their first year, and 80% by age 5. Many patients develop allergic rhinitis or asthma (approximately 80%).
keyPoints: Pruritus and scratching are prominent features.
Course: marked by exacerbations and remissions; lesions last >6 weeks.
Age of onset: 50% within the first year, 80% by age 5.
Coexpression with allergic rhinitis or asthma in about 80% of patients.

items.title: Topical Treatment Strategy for Atopic Dermatitis
items.content: The management of atopic dermatitis (AD) involves avoiding irritants, moisturizing with emollients, and using topical anti-inflammatory agents. Low- to mid-potency glucocorticoids are typically used, but nonglucocorticoid options like tacrolimus, pimecrolimus, crisaborole, and ruxolitinib are preferred for sensitive areas due to their safety profile.
keyPoints: Approximately 30 g of topical agent is needed to cover an adult's body surface.
Low-potency glucocorticoids or nonglucocorticoid agents should be used on the face and intertriginous areas.
Nonglucocorticoid options include tacrolimus, pimecrolimus (TCIs), crisaborole (phosphodiesterase-4 inhibitor), and ruxolitinib (JAK inhibitor).
Concerns about lymphomas with TCIs are largely unfounded.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · Atopic Dermatitis

items.title: Management of Secondary Infections in Atopic Dermatitis
items.content: Secondary infections, particularly with S. aureus, can exacerbate AD. Culturing lesions and using systemic antibiotics like penicillinase-resistant penicillins or cephalosporins is recommended. For CA-MRSA, options include trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole, minocycline, doxycycline, or clindamycin. Adjuncts like antibacterial washes and nasal mupirocin may be beneficial.
keyPoints: Secondary infection can exacerbate AD; culture lesions when suspected.
Initial antibiotics: penicillinase-resistant penicillins or cephalosporins (e.g., dicloxacillin, cephaloxin 250 mg qid for 7–10 days).
For CA-MRSA: trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole, minocycline, doxycycline, clindamycin.
Duration of therapy should be 7–10 days; check for inducible resistance with double-disk diffusion test.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · Atopic Dermatitis

items.title: Systemic Treatment Considerations in Severe Atopic Dermatitis
items.content: Systemic glucocorticoids are reserved for severe AD exacerbations unresponsive to topical treatments. For chronic, severe cases, systemic agents like cyclosporine or biologics such as dupilumab (IL-4 receptor blocker) and tralokinumab (IL-13 inhibitor) may be considered. JAK inhibitors like abrocitinib and upadacitinib are also FDA approved for moderate to severe AD.
keyPoints: Systemic glucocorticoids should be limited to severe exacerbations.
Cyclosporine requires monitoring of renal function and secondary infections.
Dupilumab (IL-4 receptor blocker) and tralokinumab (IL-13 inhibitor) approved for AD.
Abrocitinib and upadacitinib are JAK inhibitors approved for moderate to severe AD.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · Atopic Dermatitis

items.title: Breaking the Cycle: Treatment of Lichen Simplex Chronicus
items.content: Lichen simplex chronicus is a condition characterized by lichenified skin due to persistent scratching or rubbing, often seen in areas like the posterior nuchal region and dorsum of feet. The primary treatment goal is to interrupt this cycle of itching and scratching.
keyPoints: Commonly affects areas such as the posterior nuchal region, dorsum of the feet, and ankles.
Treatment involves high-potency topical glucocorticoids; in recalcitrant cases, occlusion or intralesional injections may be necessary.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · LICHEN SIMPLEX CHRONICUS | CONTACT DERMATITIS

items.title: Understanding Contact Dermatitis: ICD vs. ACD
items.content: Contact dermatitis is an inflammatory skin condition caused by exogenous agents, categorized into irritant contact dermatitis (ICD) and allergic contact dermatitis (ACD). ICD results from direct chemical injury, while ACD involves a delayed-type hypersensitivity reaction.
keyPoints: ICD lesions are well demarcated and can develop within minutes to hours; common in areas of thin skin or where irritants were occluded.
Chronic low-grade ICD is most common on the hands due to exposure to soaps, detergents, and wet work.
ACD requires prior sensitization and manifests 12-72 hours after exposure, with urushiol from plants like poison ivy being a frequent cause.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · LICHEN SIMPLEX CHRONICUS | CONTACT DERMATITIS

items.title: Management and Systemic Therapy for Contact Dermatitis
items.content: When contact dermatitis is suspected, removing the offending agent typically leads to resolution. High-potency topical glucocorticoids are usually sufficient for symptom relief. In cases requiring systemic therapy, oral prednisone at 1 mg/kg (≤60 mg/d) taken in the morning with food can be used and should be tapered over 2–3 weeks.
keyPoints: Resolution of contact dermatitis often occurs upon removal of the offending agent.
High-potency topical glucocorticoids are typically sufficient for treatment.
Systemic therapy may involve oral prednisone at 1 mg/kg, usually ≤60 mg/d.
Prednisone should be tapered over 2–3 weeks and taken in the morning with food.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · Contact Dermatitis | HAND ECZEMA

items.title: Diagnosis and Evaluation of Hand Eczema
items.content: Hand eczema is a chronic skin disorder influenced by both exogenous and endogenous factors. It often presents with dryness, cracking, erythema, and edema, frequently under rings where irritants are trapped. Dyshidrotic eczema is a variant characterized by pruritic papules and vesicles on the hands. Evaluation should focus on occupational exposures to identify potential irritant or allergen triggers.
keyPoints: Hand eczema may be associated with other cutaneous disorders like atopic dermatitis (AD).
Common presentations include dryness, cracking, erythema, and edema of the hands.
Dyshidrotic eczema presents with pruritic papules and vesicles on the thenar and hypothenar eminences.
Evaluation should assess occupational exposures to identify potential irritants or allergens.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · Contact Dermatitis | HAND ECZEMA

items.subtopic: Hand Eczema | NUMMULAR ECZEMA | ASTEATOTIC ECZEMA
items.title: Management of Hand Eczema: Avoidance and Treatment Strategies
items.content: Hand eczema management focuses on avoiding irritants, identifying contact allergens, treating coexistent infections, and using topical glucocorticoids. Protective gloves are recommended, with a preference for vinyl over latex to prevent hypersensitivity reactions.
keyPoints: Avoidance of irritants is crucial in managing hand eczema.
Identify possible contact allergens to tailor treatment.
Use mid- to high-potency topical glucocorticoids for symptom relief.
Protect hands with gloves, preferably vinyl, to prevent hypersensitivity reactions associated with latex or rubber accelerators.
Treat secondary infections and examine for dermatophyte infection using KOH preparation and culture.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · Hand Eczema | NUMMULAR ECZEMA | ASTEATOTIC ECZEMA

items.subtopic: Hand Eczema | NUMMULAR ECZEMA | ASTEATOTIC ECZEMA
items.title: Characteristics and Management of Nummular and Asteatotic Eczema
items.content: Nummular eczema presents as circular, coin-like lesions on the trunk or extremities, often exacerbated by dry skin. It is more common in middle-aged men. Asteatotic eczema, or 'winter itch,' occurs in areas of extremely dry skin and responds well to moisturizers.
keyPoints: Nummular eczema features circular lesions that are crusted and scaly, often on the trunk or extremities.
Dry skin is a contributing factor for nummular eczema.
Asteatotic eczema manifests as fine cracks and scale in dry areas, particularly on lower extremities of elderly patients.
Both conditions respond to topical moisturizers and avoidance of irritants.
Overbathing and harsh soaps can exacerbate asteatotic eczema.

items.subtopic: STASIS DERMATITIS AND STASIS ULCERATION | Stasis Dermatitis and Stasis Ulceration
items.title: Differentiating Stasis Dermatitis from Cellulitis
items.content: Stasis dermatitis, often resulting from venous incompetence, presents with symmetrical erythema and scaling on the lower extremities. It can be confused with cellulitis but is distinguished by its bilateral nature and chronic progression to pigmentation due to hemosiderin deposition.
keyPoints: Symmetrical and bilateral involvement suggests stasis dermatitis over cellulitis.
Chronic erythrocyte extravasation leads to cutaneous hemosiderin deposition, causing pigmentation.
Stasis dermatitis can precede stasis ulcers.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · STASIS DERMATITIS AND STASIS ULCERATION | Stasis Dermatitis and Stasis Ulceration

items.subtopic: STASIS DERMATITIS AND STASIS ULCERATION | Stasis Dermatitis and Stasis Ulceration
items.title: Management of Stasis Dermatitis and Ulceration
items.content: Effective management of stasis dermatitis involves leg elevation, compression stockings with at least 30–40 mmHg gradient, and use of emollients or mid-potency topical glucocorticoids. For stasis ulcers, elevation, gentle debridement, semipermeable dressings, and avoiding glucocorticoid application on ulcers are crucial.
keyPoints: Compression stockings should have a gradient of at least 30–40 mmHg for efficacy.
Glucocorticoids can be used on surrounding skin but not directly on stasis ulcers to avoid delayed healing.
Exclude treatable causes like hypercoagulation, vasculitis, and arterial insufficiency before chronic management.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · STASIS DERMATITIS AND STASIS ULCERATION | Stasis Dermatitis and Stasis Ulceration

items.title: Recognizing and Treating Seborrheic Dermatitis
items.content: Seborrheic dermatitis is a chronic skin condition marked by greasy scales over erythematous patches, commonly affecting the scalp as severe dandruff. It can also appear on the face, chest, axilla, groin, and gluteal cleft. The condition may present in infancy (cradle cap) and re-emerge during adolescence or adulthood. While it is more frequent in patients with Parkinson's disease, cerebrovascular accidents, or HIV infection, most cases occur without underlying disorders.
keyPoints: Characterized by greasy scales over erythematous patches
Common locations: scalp (severe dandruff), face (eyebrows, eyelids, glabella, nasolabial folds), chest, axilla, groin, gluteal cleft
May present as cradle cap in infants and re-emerge during adolescence or adulthood
More frequent in patients with Parkinson's disease, cerebrovascular accidents, or HIV infection
Treatment: low-potency topical glucocorticoids + antifungal agent (ketoconazole or ciclopirox cream)
Antidandruff shampoos for scalp and beard areas; leave 3–5 min before rinsing
High-potency glucocorticoids like betamethasone or clobetasol for severe scalp involvement, but avoid on face due to risk of steroid-induced rosacea or atrophy
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · SEBORRHEIC DERMATITIS | Seborrheic Dermatitis

items.title: Management Strategies for Seborrheic Dermatitis
items.content: Effective management of seborrheic dermatitis involves a combination of topical treatments. Low-potency glucocorticoids and antifungal agents are typically used to control symptoms, while antidandruff shampoos can be beneficial for scalp involvement. High-potency glucocorticoid solutions are reserved for severe cases but should not be applied to the face due to potential adverse effects like rosacea or atrophy.
keyPoints: Treatment involves low-potency topical glucocorticoids and antifungal agents (ketoconazole or ciclopirox cream)
Antidandruff shampoos for scalp, leave on 3–5 min before rinsing
High-potency solutions like betamethasone or clobetasol for severe cases
Avoid high-potency glucocorticoids on the face to prevent steroid-induced rosacea or atrophy

items.title: Understanding Psoriasis Prevalence and Clinical Presentation
items.content: Psoriasis is a common dermatologic condition affecting up to 2% of the global population. It manifests as erythematous, sharply demarcated papules and plaques covered by silvery scales, often pruritic. The Koebner phenomenon indicates lesion development in traumatized areas.
keyPoints: Psoriasis affects up to 2% of the world's population.
Clinical features include erythematous, sharply demarcated papules and plaques with silvery scale.
Lesions are variably pruritic; Koebner phenomenon can occur.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · PSORIASIS

items.title: Recognizing Psoriasis Variants and Associated Conditions
items.content: Plaque-type psoriasis is the most common form, affecting areas like elbows, knees, gluteal cleft, and scalp. Guttate psoriasis often follows upper respiratory infections with β-hemolytic streptococci. Pustular psoriasis can be localized or generalized, with systemic symptoms including fever (39°–40°C). Psoriatic arthritis affects up to 30% of patients.
keyPoints: Plaque-type psoriasis is most common and involves elbows, knees, gluteal cleft, scalp.
Guttate psoriasis often follows upper respiratory infections with β-hemolytic streptococci.
Pustular psoriasis can cause fever (39°–40°C) in generalized form.
Up to 30% of patients with psoriasis have psoriatic arthritis.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · PSORIASIS

items.title: Identifying Psoriasis Triggers and Treatment Options
items.content: Psoriasis can be exacerbated by infections, stress, and certain medications like lithium, beta blockers, and antimalarial drugs. Pustular psoriasis may be triggered by local irritants, pregnancy, or systemic glucocorticoid withdrawal. Oral retinoids are preferred for nonpregnant patients with pustular psoriasis.
keyPoints: Psoriasis can be exacerbated by infections, stress, lithium, beta blockers, antimalarial drugs.
Pustular psoriasis triggers include local irritants, pregnancy, glucocorticoid withdrawal.
Oral retinoids are the treatment of choice for nonpregnant patients with pustular psoriasis.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · PSORIASIS

items.title: Treatment Modalities and Considerations in Psoriasis Management
items.content: The management of psoriasis varies based on type, location, and extent. Topical treatments like mid-potency glucocorticoids are common for localized plaque-type psoriasis but may lead to tachyphylaxis and skin atrophy with long-term use. Vitamin D analogues (calcipotriene) and retinoids (tazarotene) are preferred alternatives for limited psoriasis. UV light therapy, including UV-B and PUVA, is effective for widespread disease due to its immunosuppressive properties but carries a risk of skin cancer. Systemic treatments like methotrexate, acitretin, apremilast, and deucravacitinib are used for severe cases, with specific considerations such as teratogenicity and renal function.
keyPoints: Avoid excess drying or irritation; maintain hydration.
Mid-potency glucocorticoids can cause tachyphylaxis and skin atrophy.
Calcipotriene and tazarotene are preferred for limited psoriasis.
UV light therapy (UV-B, PUVA) is effective but increases skin cancer risk.
Systemic agents: Methotrexate (effective in PsA), Acitretin (teratogenicity concern), Apremilast (caution with renal failure/depression), Deucravacitinib (IL-23/12 inhibition).
Immunosuppressive agents like cyclosporine and biologics are effective but require caution in infections, CHF, and malignancies.
TNF-alpha inhibitors may worsen CHF; monitor for skin cancer.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · Psoriasis

items.title: Safety Considerations with Immunosuppressive Therapy in Psoriasis
items.content: Immunosuppressive therapies, including biologics and systemic agents like cyclosporine, are effective for psoriasis but require careful patient selection. TNF-alpha inhibitors should be used cautiously in patients with or at risk for congestive heart failure (CHF). Immunosuppressive treatments should not be initiated if there is a severe infection, such as tuberculosis or HIV, and routine screening for tuberculosis is recommended during therapy. There are risks of progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy, lupus erythematosus, and malignancies associated with these therapies, necessitating careful monitoring.
keyPoints: Avoid immunosuppressive agents in severe infections (e.g., TB, HIV).
TNF-alpha inhibitors may exacerbate CHF; use cautiously.
Routine tuberculosis screening is recommended during therapy.
Risks include progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy and lupus erythematosus.
Immunosuppressive agents linked to increased skin cancer risk.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · Psoriasis

items.title: Diagnosis and Management of Lichen Planus
items.content: Lichen planus (LP) is a papulosquamous disorder characterized by pruritic, polygonal, flat-topped violaceous papules with Wickham's striae. It can affect the skin, scalp, nails, and mucous membranes. LP may lead to scarring alopecia or permanent nail deformity. Mucosal involvement, particularly erosive stomatitis, poses a risk for oral squamous cell carcinoma. The course is variable, but spontaneous remissions typically occur 6 months to 2 years after onset.
keyPoints: Primary lesions: pruritic, polygonal, flat-topped, violaceous papules with Wickham's striae
Common sites: wrists, shins, lower back, genitalia; scalp (lichen planopilaris) and nails can be involved
Mucosal involvement risk: increased oral squamous cell carcinoma risk with erosive stomatitis
Course: spontaneous remissions usually occur 6 months to 2 years after onset
Treatment: topical glucocorticoids
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · LICHEN PLANUS | PITYRIASIS ROSEA

items.title: Characteristics and Management of Pityriasis Rosea
items.content: Pityriasis rosea (PR) is a papulosquamous eruption with unknown etiology, often occurring in spring or fall. It starts with a herald patch followed by smaller annular lesions on the trunk. PR lesions are oval, red to brown, and have trailing scale. They last 3–8 weeks and can be pruritic. Unlike secondary syphilis, palm and sole involvement is rare.
keyPoints: Initial manifestation: 2- to 6-cm herald patch
Follow-up lesions: smaller annular or papular on the trunk
Lesion characteristics: oval, red to brown, trailing scale
Duration: lasts 3–8 weeks
Treatment: oral antihistamines, mid-potency topical glucocorticoids, UV-B phototherapy
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · LICHEN PLANUS | PITYRIASIS ROSEA

items.title: Diagnosis and Management of Impetigo and Its Variants
items.content: Impetigo is a superficial bacterial skin infection primarily caused by S. aureus or group A β-hemolytic streptococci. It presents as pustules that rupture to form honey-colored crusts, with bullous impetigo being a less common variant due to exfoliative toxin production by S. aureus phage type II. Ecthyma is a deeper variant caused mainly by Streptococcus pyogenes and results in ulcerative lesions. Management involves debridement of crusts, topical and oral antibiotics.
keyPoints: Impetigo primarily caused by S. aureus or group A β-hemolytic streptococci
Characteristic honey-colored crusts from ruptured pustules
Bullous impetigo involves exfoliative toxin production by S. aureus phage type II
Ecthyma is deeper, often caused by Streptococcus pyogenes, and resolves with scarring
Management includes debridement, topical and oral antibiotics

items.title: Approach to Furunculosis in the Context of CA-MRSA
items.content: Furunculosis is a painful skin infection caused by S. aureus, with increased incidence due to CA-MRSA. It presents as erythematous nodules that may rupture and drain spontaneously or require incision and drainage. Lesional material should be cultured. Treatment for methicillin-sensitive infections involves β-lactam antibiotics, while CA-MRSA requires specific therapy including warm compresses, nasal mupirocin, and possibly IV antibiotics.
keyPoints: Furunculosis caused by S. aureus, with increased incidence from CA-MRSA
Presents as erythematous nodules that may rupture or require drainage
Culture lesional material for accurate diagnosis
Treatment: β-lactam antibiotics for methicillin-sensitive infections
CA-MRSA therapy includes warm compresses, nasal mupirocin, and possibly IV antibiotics

items.title: Recognizing and Managing Tinea Infections: Key Clinical Features
items.content: Dermatophytes cause infections in skin, hair, and nails, presenting with distinct clinical features depending on the body site. Tinea corporis appears as erythematous, scaly plaques with an annular shape. Tinea cruris is more common in males, sparing the scrotum. Tinea pedis often involves the web space between the fourth and fifth toes and can be chronic. Onychomycosis presents with opacified, thickened nails, and proximal subungual involvement may indicate immunocompromise.
keyPoints: Dermatophytes include Trichophyton, Microsporum, Epidermophyton.
Tinea corporis: erythematous, scaly plaques with annular appearance.
Tinea cruris more common in males; spares the scrotum.
Tinea pedis often involves web space between fourth and fifth toes.
Onychomycosis: opacified, thickened nails; proximal subungual variant may indicate immunocompromise.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · DERMATOPHYTOSIS

items.title: Diagnostic Approach to Dermatophyte Infections
items.content: Diagnosis of tinea infections is confirmed through skin, nail, or hair scrapings. KOH examination and culture are standard diagnostic methods. Nail clippings can be examined histologically with PAS stain for onychomycosis. Recognizing the clinical presentation aids in selecting appropriate specimens for diagnosis.
keyPoints: Diagnosis via skin/nail/hair scrapings.
KOH examination and culture used for diagnosis.
PAS stain for histologic examination of nail clippings.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · DERMATOPHYTOSIS

items.title: When to Use Oral Antifungal Agents in Dermatophyte Infections
items.content: Oral antifungal agents are indicated for dermatophyte infections that involve the hair and nails or when topical therapies fail. Before prescribing, fungal etiology should be confirmed by direct microscopic examination or culture.
keyPoints: Topical therapy is effective for uncomplicated tinea corporis, cruris, and limited pedis.
Oral antifungals are required for recalcitrant infections or those involving hair/nails.
Fungal etiology confirmation via direct microscopic examination or culture is necessary before prescribing oral agents.
All oral antifungals may cause hepatotoxicity; avoid in pregnancy and breastfeeding.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · Dermatophytosis

items.title: Considerations for Specific Oral Antifungal Agents
items.content: Griseofulvin, itraconazole, and terbinafine are oral antifungals used in dermatophyte infections. Each has specific indications and potential side effects or drug interactions.
keyPoints: Griseofulvin is approved for skin, hair, or nail infections; common side effects include gastrointestinal distress, headache, urticaria.
Itraconazole is approved for onychomycosis but can interact with drugs metabolized by P450 and should not be used in ventricular dysfunction/CHF.
Terbinafine is approved for onychomycosis and tinea capitis; fewer drug interactions than itraconazole.
Ketoconazole oral tablets are not recommended as first-line treatment due to hepatotoxicity risk.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · Dermatophytosis

items.title: Diagnosis and Treatment of Tinea Versicolor
items.content: Tinea versicolor is a superficial fungal infection caused by Malassezia furfur, exacerbated by heat and humidity. It presents as scaly macules on the chest, shoulders, and back. Diagnosis involves identifying 'spaghetti and meatballs' in KOH preparation from scaling lesions.
keyPoints: Caused by Malassezia furfur
Promoted by heat and humidity
Lesions: oval scaly macules, papules, patches on chest, shoulders, back
KOH prep shows 'spaghetti and meatballs'
Treatment: topical sulfur, salicylic acid, or selenium sulfide for 1–2 weeks daily then weekly
Oral antifungals are effective but not FDA approved
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · TINEA (PITYRIASIS) VERSICOLOR | ☐ CANDIDIASIS

items.title: Recognition and Management of Candidiasis
items.content: Candidiasis is caused by Candida species, often Candida albicans. It can be localized or systemic, especially in immunocompromised individuals like those with HIV. Oral thrush presents as white plaques on the tongue or buccal mucosa.
keyPoints: Caused by Candida species, usually C. albicans
Common in HIV-infected individuals
Oral involvement: white plaques (thrush) on tongue/buccal mucosa
Intertriginous lesions: edematous, erythematous, scaly with satellite pustules
Painful and inflammatory compared to dermatophyte infections
Diagnosis by clinical pattern and KOH prep or culture
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · TINEA (PITYRIASIS) VERSICOLOR | ☐ CANDIDIASIS

items.title: Management of Cutaneous Candidiasis: Topical vs. Systemic Therapy
items.content: Cutaneous candidiasis is treated by addressing predisposing factors and using antifungal agents. Effective topical treatments include nystatin or azoles such as miconazole, clotrimazole, econazole, or ketoconazole. For inflammatory symptoms on glabrous skin, a mild glucocorticoid lotion (2.5% hydrocortisone) can be used. Systemic therapy with oral fluconazole is reserved for immunosuppressed patients or those with chronic/recurrent disease unresponsive to topical treatments.
keyPoints: Effective topicals: nystatin, azoles (miconazole, clotrimazole, econazole, ketoconazole)
Mild glucocorticoid lotion/cream for inflammation: 2.5% hydrocortisone
Systemic therapy with oral fluconazole for immunosuppressed or chronic/recurrent cases
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · Candidiasis | WARTS

items.title: HPV-Related Wart Types and Associated Risks
items.content: Warts are caused by human papillomaviruses (HPVs), with over 100 types identified. Common wart types include verruca vulgaris, plant warts, filiform warts, and flat warts. Genital warts can involve various genital areas and mucosal surfaces. HPV types 16 and 18 are major risk factors for intraepithelial neoplasia and squamous cell carcinoma of the cervix, anus, vulva, and penis. Immunocompromised individuals, such as those with HIV or post-transplantation, have a higher risk.
keyPoints: Over 100 HPV types identified
HPV types 16 and 18: major risk factors for intraepithelial neoplasia and squamous cell carcinoma of cervix, anus, vulva, penis
Higher risk in immunocompromised individuals (HIV, post-transplantation)
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · Candidiasis | WARTS

items.title: Management of Warts: Spontaneous Resolution and Treatment Options
items.content: Most warts in normal individuals resolve spontaneously within 1–2 years. Various treatment modalities exist, but no single therapy is universally effective. Cryotherapy with liquid nitrogen is a convenient method for treating non-genital warts, while keratolytic agents like salicylic acid require more patient compliance.
keyPoints: Most warts resolve spontaneously within 1–2 years.
Cryotherapy with liquid nitrogen is useful for non-genital warts.
Keratolytic agents (e.g., salicylic acid) are effective but need high patient compliance.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · Warts | HERPES SIMPLEX | ACNE VULGARIS

items.title: Acne Vulgaris: Clinical Presentation and Impact on Quality of Life
items.content: Acne vulgaris primarily affects teenagers and young adults, with 10–20% of adults experiencing some form. It is characterized by comedones (closed or open) and inflammatory lesions like papules, pustules, or nodules. While most cases are mild, severe acne can lead to significant scarring.
keyPoints: Acne vulgaris affects 10–20% of adults.
Comedones (whiteheads/blackheads) are the clinical hallmark.
Severe acne may result in significant scarring.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · Warts | HERPES SIMPLEX | ACNE VULGARIS

items.title: Factors Influencing Acne Vulgaris Expression and Management
items.content: Exogenous factors like friction, comedogenic agents, and certain medications can exacerbate acne. Endogenous factors include genetic predisposition and polycystic ovary disease. Early intervention is crucial for severe cases to prevent permanent scarring.
keyPoints: Friction and comedogenic agents can worsen acne.
Medications like glucocorticoids may elicit acneiform eruptions.
Early treatment is essential in severe, scarring acne.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · Warts | HERPES SIMPLEX | ACNE VULGARIS

items.title: Approach to Topical Treatment of Acne Vulgaris
items.content: Topical treatments for acne vulgaris aim to normalize follicular keratinization, reduce sebaceous gland activity, decrease C. acnes population, and manage inflammation. Agents like retinoic acid, benzoyl peroxide, or salicylic acid modify epidermal desquamation patterns, preventing comedone formation and aiding cyst resolution. Topical antibiotics (erythromycin, clindamycin) should be combined with benzoyl peroxide to prevent bacterial resistance.
keyPoints: Topical agents: retinoic acid, benzoyl peroxide, salicylic acid
Prevent comedone formation and aid cyst resolution
Use topical antibiotics (erythromycin, clindamycin) with benzoyl peroxide to prevent resistance
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · Acne Vulgaris

items.title: Management of Severe Nodulocystic Acne with Isotretinoin
items.content: Isotretinoin is recommended for severe nodulocystic acne unresponsive to other treatments. It requires weight-based, cumulative dosing, and therapy duration depends on the summative dose or lesion count. Due to its teratogenic potential, strict regulatory measures are in place, including pregnancy prevention programs for prescribers and patients.
keyPoints: Indicated for severe nodulocystic acne unresponsive to other treatments
Weight-based, cumulative dosing; duration based on summative dose or lesion count
Strict regulations due to teratogenicity: Pregnancy prevention program required
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · Acne Vulgaris

items.subtopic: ACNE ROSACEA | Acne Rosacea | Skin Manifestations of Internal Disease
items.title: Recognizing and Managing Acne Rosacea: Key Features and Treatment Approaches
items.content: Acne rosacea is an inflammatory facial disorder primarily affecting adults, particularly those with a northern European background. It manifests as erythema, telangiectasias, and superficial pustules without comedones. The condition often begins with pronounced flushing due to various stimuli and can progress to persistent erythema with papules and pustules. Severe cases may involve rhinophyma or ocular complications requiring ophthalmologic evaluation.
keyPoints: Primarily affects adults, rarely <30 years old
More common in women but more severe in men
Characterized by erythema, telangiectasias, and superficial pustules without comedones
Flushing can be triggered by heat, emotions, alcohol, hot drinks, or spicy foods
Persistent erythema may lead to rhinophyma and ocular complications like keratitis, blepharitis, iritis, recurrent chalazion
Topical treatments include metronidazole, sodium sulfacetamide, azelaic acid, ivermectin, brimonidine, oxymetazoline; severe cases may require oral tetracyclines or low-dose isotretinoin
Avoid topical glucocorticoids due to potential exacerbation of rosacea
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · ACNE ROSACEA | Acne Rosacea | Skin Manifestations of Internal Disease

items.subtopic: ACNE ROSACEA | Acne Rosacea | Skin Manifestations of Internal Disease
items.title: Distinguishing Cutaneous Signs of Internal Diseases: The Role of Dermatology in Diagnosis
items.content: The skin can reflect internal diseases, presenting with specific signs that aid in diagnosis. Nondermatologists may find it challenging to recognize these cutaneous manifestations and their implications for systemic disorders. This chapter emphasizes the importance of identifying clinical signs and symptoms indicative of underlying conditions rather than focusing solely on individual diseases.
keyPoints: Skin can exhibit signs of internal disease
Nondermatologists often struggle with recognizing cutaneous lesions related to systemic disorders
Focus is on clinical signs pointing to specific disorders, not just individual diseases
Differentiate significant diseases from common cutaneous disorders with minimal internal significance
