items.subtopic: PAPULOSQUAMOUS SKIN LESIONS | TABLE 61-1 Selected Causes of Papulosquamous Skin Lesions
items.title: Recognizing Psoriasis and Its Comorbidities
items.content: Psoriasis is a common papulosquamous disorder that can be exacerbated by certain medications, infections, or conditions. It's important to consider comorbidities such as cardiovascular disease and metabolic syndrome in patients with psoriasis.
keyPoints: Psoriasis may be exacerbated by lithium, beta blockers, anti-PD-1/PD-L1 antibodies, HIV, streptococcal infections, and rapid taper of systemic glucocorticoids.
TNF inhibitors can paradoxically induce psoriatic lesions.
Comorbidities in patients with psoriasis include cardiovascular disease and metabolic syndrome.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · PAPULOSQUAMOUS SKIN LESIONS | TABLE 61-1 Selected Causes of Papulosquamous Skin Lesions

items.subtopic: PAPULOSQUAMOUS SKIN LESIONS | TABLE 61-1 Selected Causes of Papulosquamous Skin Lesions
items.title: Differential Diagnosis: Psoriatic Arthritis vs. Reactive Arthritis
items.content: When psoriatic lesions are accompanied by arthritis, consider the possibility of psoriatic arthritis or reactive arthritis. A history of oral ulcers, conjunctivitis, uveitis, and/or urethritis suggests reactive arthritis.
keyPoints: Psoriatic lesions with arthritis may indicate psoriatic arthritis or reactive arthritis.
Oral ulcers, conjunctivitis, uveitis, and urethritis are red flags for reactive arthritis.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · PAPULOSQUAMOUS SKIN LESIONS | TABLE 61-1 Selected Causes of Papulosquamous Skin Lesions

items.subtopic: PAPULOSQUAMOUS SKIN LESIONS | TABLE 61-1 Selected Causes of Papulosquamous Skin Lesions
items.title: Identifying Mycosis Fungoides in Early Stages
items.content: Mycosis fungoides (MF), a form of cutaneous T-cell lymphoma, can mimic eczema or psoriasis early on. Diagnosis is confirmed by skin biopsy showing atypical T lymphocytes and supported by TCR gene rearrangements.
keyPoints: Early MF may be confused with eczema or psoriasis but fails to respond to their treatments.
Diagnosis of MF involves finding atypical T lymphocytes in the epidermis and dermis via skin biopsy.
TCR gene rearrangements support the diagnosis of MF.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · PAPULOSQUAMOUS SKIN LESIONS | TABLE 61-1 Selected Causes of Papulosquamous Skin Lesions

items.title: Diagnosing Psoriasis in Erythroderma: Recognizing Initial Lesions
items.content: In cases of erythroderma, identifying the initial lesions can guide diagnosis. A history of red scaly plaques on elbows and knees suggests psoriasis as a primary cause.
keyPoints: Psoriasis is a common etiology for erythroderma.
Initial presentation often includes red scaly plaques on elbows and knees.
Careful examination may reveal migratory waves of erythema with superficial pustules, characteristic of pustular psoriasis.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · ERYTHRODERMA | TABLE 61-2 Causes of Erythroderma

items.title: Identifying Drug-Induced Erythroderma: Recognizing DRESS Syndrome
items.content: Drug-induced erythroderma can manifest as an exanthematous eruption or diffuse erythema. It is often associated with systemic symptoms like fever, peripheral eosinophilia, and facial swelling.
keyPoints: Common drugs causing erythroderma include penicillins, sulfonamides, aromatic anticonvulsants (e.g., carbamazepine, phenytoin), and allopurinol.
DRESS syndrome is characterized by fever, peripheral eosinophilia, facial swelling, hepatitis, allergic interstitial nephritis, myocarditis, and delayed thyroiditis.
Reactions to aromatic anticonvulsants can lead to pseudolymphoma syndrome with adenopathy and circulating atypical lymphocytes.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · ERYTHRODERMA | TABLE 61-2 Causes of Erythroderma

items.title: Understanding Nonscarring vs. Scarring Alopecia
items.content: Nonscarring alopecia involves hair shaft absence or miniaturization without follicle loss, allowing reversibility. In contrast, scarring alopecia results in fibrosis and permanent follicular loss.
keyPoints: Nonscarring alopecia preserves hair follicles; scarring does not.
Common causes of nonscarring include androgenetic alopecia, telogen effluvium, alopecia areata, tinea capitis, and early traumatic alopecia.
Scarring alopecia is often due to primary cutaneous disorders like lichen planus or discoid lupus.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · TABLE 61-4 Causes of Alopecia | ALOPECIA

items.title: Drug-induced Alopecia: Telogen vs. Anagen Effluvium
items.content: Drugs can induce alopecia by causing telogen effluvium, with spontaneous regrowth post-discontinuation, or anagen effluvium from chemotherapeutic agents like anthracyclines.
keyPoints: Telogen effluvium is common drug-induced alopecia; anagen effluvium occurs with chemotherapy (e.g., daunorubicin).
Drugs causing telogen effluvium include warfarin, heparin, isotretinoin, and lithium.
Spontaneous regrowth typically follows cessation of the offending drug.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · TABLE 61-4 Causes of Alopecia | ALOPECIA

items.title: Systemic Diseases and Their Impact on Alopecia
items.content: Hypothyroidism, hyperthyroidism, and systemic lupus erythematosus can cause nonscarring alopecia. Scarring alopecia may result from discoid lupus or sarcoidosis.
keyPoints: Nonscarring alopecia in systemic diseases includes hypothyroidism and hyperthyroidism.
Systemic lupus erythematosus presents with both scarring (discoid lesions) and nonscarring forms.
Scarring alopecia can be due to discoid lupus or sarcoidosis.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · TABLE 61-4 Causes of Alopecia | ALOPECIA

items.title: Erythema Migrans: Recognizing Lyme Disease Early
items.content: Erythema migrans is the hallmark cutaneous manifestation of early Lyme disease, caused by Borrelia burgdorferi. It typically appears as a single annular lesion that can expand significantly in size following an unnoticed tick bite.
keyPoints: Occurs 3–30 days after a tick bite.
Initial lesion diameter can be ≥10 cm.
Primary erythema migrans (as defined by the CDC) has a diameter of ≥5 cm.
Up to half of patients may develop multiple smaller lesions at sites distant from the bite within several days.
Associated symptoms include fever, headache, photophobia, myalgias, arthralgias, and malar rash.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · FIGURATE SKIN LESIONS | ACNE

items.title: Erythema Gyratum Repens: Link to Underlying Malignancy
items.content: Erythema gyratum repens is characterized by mobile concentric arcs and wavefronts, resembling wood grain. It necessitates a thorough search for an underlying malignancy in affected patients.
keyPoints: Lesions appear as numerous mobile concentric arcs and wavefronts.
Mandatory evaluation for underlying malignancy.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · FIGURATE SKIN LESIONS | ACNE

items.title: Identifying Pathogens in Follicular Pustules and Recognizing Noninfectious Causes
items.content: Follicular pustules can be caused by various pathogens or noninfectious factors. Identifying the pathogen is crucial for appropriate treatment, while recognizing noninfectious causes like drug reactions or systemic diseases helps in managing underlying conditions.
keyPoints: Common follicular pustular dermatoses include acneiform eruptions and folliculitis.

Pathogens associated with follicular pustules: normal flora (culture-negative), Staphylococcus aureus, Pseudomonas aeruginosa ('hot tub' folliculitis), Malassezia, dermatophytes (Majocchi's granuloma), Demodex spp.
Noninfectious causes include HIV- or immunosuppression-associated eosinophilic folliculitis and drug-induced folliculitis from glucocorticoids, lithium, EGFR inhibitors, or MEK inhibitors.
Systemic diseases with nonfollicular-based pustules: pustular psoriasis (sterile) and septic emboli of bacterial or fungal origin.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · PUSTULAR LESIONS | TELANGIECTASIAS

items.title: Characteristics and Causes of Telangiectasias in Various Conditions
items.content: Telangiectasias vary by shape, configuration, and associated conditions. Linear telangiectasias appear on the face with actinic damage or acne rosacea and on legs with venous hypertension. Mat telangiectasias are specific to systemic sclerosis.
keyPoints: Linear telangiectasias: seen in actinically damaged skin, acne rosacea, venous hypertension, generalized essential telangiectasia, mastocytosis (telangiectasia macularis eruptiva perstans), carcinoid syndrome, and areas of cutaneous inflammation.

Poikiloderma features reticulated hypo- and hyperpigmentation, wrinkling from epidermal atrophy, and telangiectasias; associated with ionizing radiation damage, autoimmune connective tissue diseases (e.g., dermatomyositis), and rare genodermatoses like Kindler syndrome.
Mat telangiectasias in systemic sclerosis: broad macules measuring 2–7 mm, polygonal or oval shape, erythematous color.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · PUSTULAR LESIONS | TELANGIECTASIAS

items.title: Telangiectasias as a Diagnostic Clue in Systemic Sclerosis and Autoimmune Diseases
items.content: Telangiectasias are small dilated blood vessels that appear on the skin's surface. They serve as important diagnostic indicators for systemic sclerosis, particularly its limited form known as CREST syndrome. Additionally, proximal nailfold telangiectasias are pathognomonic signs of major autoimmune connective tissue diseases such as lupus erythematosus, systemic sclerosis, and dermatomyositis (DM). These can be observed in at least two-thirds of patients with these conditions.
keyPoints: Telangiectasias are common on the face, oral mucosa, and hands due to intermittent ischemia.
In CREST syndrome, telangiectasias may be the only cutaneous finding.
Proximal nailfold telangiectasias occur in at least two-thirds of patients with lupus erythematosus, systemic sclerosis, and DM.
Under 10x magnification or dermoscopy: Lupus shows tortuous vessels resembling 'glomeruli'; systemic sclerosis and DM show loss of capillary loops and marked dilation.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · TABLE 61-8 Causes of Telangiectasias

items.title: Hereditary Hemorrhagic Telangiectasia: Clinical Features and Implications
items.content: Hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia (Osler-Rendu-Weber disease) is characterized by arteriovenous malformations in the dermal microvasculature, leading to mucosal and cutaneous lesions. These lesions are typically dark red, slightly elevated, and prone to bleeding due to their nature as AV communications. They commonly appear during adolescence on mucosal surfaces and adulthood on the skin.
keyPoints: Lesions in hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia appear during adolescence (mucosal) and adulthood (cutaneous).
Common locations include nasal, orolabial mucosa, face, and distal extremities.
Lesions are dark red, slightly elevated AV malformations of the dermal microvasculature.
Major symptoms include recurrent epistaxis and gastrointestinal bleeding due to AV communications.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · TABLE 61-8 Causes of Telangiectasias

items.title: Understanding Oculocutaneous Albinism (OCA) and its Genetic Basis
items.content: Oculocutaneous albinism is a classic example of diffuse hypopigmentation, primarily caused by mutations in the tyrosinase gene (type I) or the OCA2 gene (type II). Type IA OCA results from a total lack of enzyme activity. While all forms may appear similar at birth with white hair, gray-blue eyes, and pink-white skin, those without tyrosinase activity maintain this phenotype throughout life. Others with decreased activity will gain some pigmentation as they age.
keyPoints: Oculocutaneous albinism (OCA) is caused by mutations in the tyrosinase gene (type I) or OCA2 gene (type II).
Type IA OCA results from a total lack of enzyme activity.
Patients with no tyrosinase activity maintain their phenotype throughout life, while those with decreased activity acquire pigmentation over time.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · HYPOPIGMENTATION

items.title: Vitiligo and its Association with Autoimmune Disorders
items.content: Localized hypomelanosis includes disorders like vitiligo, which is associated with an increased incidence of autoimmune conditions. Up to 30% of patients with vitiligo have thyroid gland diseases. Common circulating autoantibodies in these patients include antithyroglobulin, antimicrosomal, and antithyroid-stimulating hormone receptor antibodies.
keyPoints: Vitiligo is associated with an increased incidence of autoimmune disorders.
Thyroid gland diseases occur in up to 30% of vitiligo patients.
Common autoantibodies include antithyroglobulin, antimicrosomal, and antithyroid-stimulating hormone receptor antibodies.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · HYPOPIGMENTATION

items.title: Vitiligo-like Leukoderma in Systemic Sclerosis
items.content: Systemic sclerosis can cause vitiligo-like leukoderma, which resembles repigmented idiopathic vitiligo. Unlike idiopathic vitiligo, it often starts on the upper trunk and extensor forearms. This condition lacks inflammation but may resolve if systemic disease activity decreases.
keyPoints: Vitiligo-like leukoderma in systemic sclerosis can mimic repigmented idiopathic vitiligo with perifollicular macules of normal pigmentation within depigmented areas.
Typically begins on the upper trunk and extensor forearms, unlike idiopathic vitiligo.
Resolution may occur if underlying connective tissue disease becomes inactive.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · TABLE 61-9 Causes of Hypopigmentation (part 1)

items.title: Melanoma-associated Vitiligo-like Leukoderma
items.content: Vitiligo-like leukoderma associated with melanoma may indicate metastatic disease or result from immunotherapy. It often begins on the upper trunk and extensor forearms, prompting a search for underlying malignancy.
keyPoints: May begin spontaneously on the upper trunk and extensor forearms.
Associated with immunotherapy for melanoma, including immune checkpoint inhibitors.
Presence suggests potential metastatic disease; warrants investigation.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · TABLE 61-9 Causes of Hypopigmentation (part 1)

items.title: Vogt-Koyanagi-Harada Syndrome and Associated Symptoms
items.content: Vogt-Koyanagi-Harada syndrome presents with hypopigmentation, often on the face and scalp. It is associated with aseptic meningitis, nontraumatic uveitis, tinnitus, hearing loss, and dysacousia.
keyPoints: Hypopigmentation commonly affects the face and scalp.
Associated symptoms include aseptic meningitis, nontraumatic uveitis, tinnitus, hearing loss, and dysacousia.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · TABLE 61-9 Causes of Hypopigmentation (part 1)

items.title: Recognizing Systemic Disease through Cutaneous Manifestations: Acanthosis Nigricans and Leser-Trélat Sign
items.content: Acanthosis nigricans can indicate systemic conditions such as internal malignancies, particularly of the gastrointestinal tract. It manifests as velvety hyperpigmentation in flexural areas. The sudden appearance of multiple seborrheic keratoses with an inflammatory base (Leser-Trélat sign) also suggests a potential underlying internal malignancy.
keyPoints: Acanthosis nigricans may reflect gastrointestinal malignancies or be associated with obesity and insulin resistance.
Leser-Trélat sign involves multiple seborrheic keratoses appearing suddenly, often with an inflammatory base, indicating possible internal malignancy.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · TABLE 61-9 Causes of Hypopigmentation (part 2) | HYPERPIGMENTATION

items.title: Identifying Systemic Syndromes through Pigmented Lesions: Lentigines in LEOPARD and Peutz-Jeghers Syndromes
items.content: Lentigines can be indicative of systemic syndromes. In LEOPARD syndrome, numerous lentigines develop during childhood across the body. In Peutz-Jeghers syndrome, lentigines are primarily around the nose, mouth, hands, feet, and oral cavity.
keyPoints: LEOPARD syndrome features hundreds of lentigines developing in childhood over the entire body surface.
Peutz-Jeghers syndrome presents with lentigines mainly around the nose, mouth, on hands and feet, and within the oral cavity.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · TABLE 61-9 Causes of Hypopigmentation (part 2) | HYPERPIGMENTATION

items.title: Significance of Multiple Café au Lait Macules in Diagnosing Neurofibromatosis
items.content: The presence of multiple or large-sized café au lait macules (CALMs) is a critical diagnostic indicator for neurofibromatosis type I (NF1). While single CALMs can be benign and present in up to 10% of the normal population, having six or more CALMs measuring ≥1.5 cm in diameter strongly suggests NF1.
keyPoints: Single CALM prevalence: up to 10% in the normal population
NF1 diagnostic threshold: >6 CALMs measuring ≥1.5 cm in diameter
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · 1. Primary cutaneous disorders (part 1)

items.title: Distinguishing Features of Café au Lait Macules in Neurofibromatosis vs. McCune-Albright Syndrome
items.content: Café au lait macules associated with neurofibromatosis type I are typically flat, uniformly brown, and smaller than those seen in McCune-Albright syndrome. In contrast, CALMs in McCune-Albright syndrome tend to be larger, more irregular, and often respect the midline.
keyPoints: NF1 CALM characteristics: flat, uniformly brown, 0.5 to 12+ cm
McCune-Albright syndrome CALM features: larger, irregular outline, respect midline
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · 1. Primary cutaneous disorders (part 1)

items.title: Pattern Recognition in Incontinentia Pigmenti and Bleomycin Pigmentation
items.content: Localized hyperpigmentation patterns can help differentiate between certain conditions. In incontinentia pigmenti, swirls and streaks are observed, whereas bleomycin pigmentation presents as flagellate streaks over joints.
keyPoints: Incontinentia pigmenti pattern: swirls and streaks
Bleomycin pigmentation pattern: flagellate streaks over elbows, knees, small hand joints
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · 1. Primary cutaneous disorders (part 1)

items.title: Recognizing Fixed Drug Eruptions
items.content: Fixed drug eruptions are characterized by recurrent circular areas of erythema that can become bullous and resolve as macular brown circles at the same location upon re-exposure to the offending agent.
keyPoints: Common medications causing fixed drug reactions include NSAIDs, sulfonamides, tetracyclines, antimalarials, and minocycline.
Typical locations for eruptions are genitalia, distal extremities, and perioral region.
Eruptions usually appear within hours of readministration.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · 1. Primary cutaneous disorders (part 2)

items.title: Understanding Diffuse Hyperpigmentation
items.content: Diffuse hyperpigmentation can be caused by endocrine, metabolic, autoimmune diseases, and certain drugs, with the skin darkening being more intense in sun-exposed areas.
keyPoints: Endocrinopathies associated include Addison's disease, Nelson's syndrome, ectopic ACTH syndrome.
Metabolic causes include porphyria cutanea tarda, hemochromatosis, and vitamin deficiencies like B12 and niacin.
Autoimmune diseases such as primary biliary cholangitis and systemic sclerosis can also cause diffuse hyperpigmentation.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · 1. Primary cutaneous disorders (part 2)

items.title: Identifying Drug-Induced Hyperpigmentation
items.content: Certain drugs, including antimalarials and minocycline, can lead to characteristic hyperpigmentation patterns.
keyPoints: Chloroquine and hydroxychloroquine cause gray-brown to blue-black discoloration on shins, hard palate, and face.
Minocycline can result in blue macules, often misdiagnosed as bruises, especially on lower extremities.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · 1. Primary cutaneous disorders (part 2)

items.title: Mechanisms of Drug-Induced Hyperpigmentation
items.content: Drug-induced hyperpigmentation can occur through various mechanisms including induction of melanin pigment formation, complexing with melanin or iron, and direct deposits in the dermis. Specific drugs like busulfan, cyclophosphamide, 5-fluorouracil, and inorganic arsenic are known to induce pigment production.
keyPoints: Mechanisms: induction of melanin formation, complexing with melanin or iron, deposits in dermis
Drugs inducing pigment production: busulfan, cyclophosphamide, 5-fluorouracil, inorganic arsenic
Complexes seen with minocycline involve melanin or iron plus the drug/metabolites
Amiodarone causes phototoxic eruption and slate-gray to violaceous discoloration
Drug deposits: silver (argyria - blue-gray), gold (chrysiasis - brown to blue-gray), clofazimine (reddish brown)
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · 1. Primary cutaneous disorders (part 3) | VESICLES/BULLAE

items.title: Classification and Characteristics of Cutaneous Blisters
items.content: Cutaneous blisters are classified based on size into vesicles (<1 cm) or bullae (>1 cm). The primary autoimmune blistering disorders include pemphigus vulgaris, pemphigus foliaceus, paraneoplastic pemphigus, bullous pemphigoid, gestational pemphigoid, cicatricial pemphigoid, epidermolysis bullosa acquisita, linear IgA bullous dermatosis (LABD), and dermatitis herpetiformis.
keyPoints: Vesicles: <1 cm
Bullae: >1 cm

Primary autoimmune blistering disorders: pemphigus vulgaris, pemphigus foliaceus, paraneoplastic pemphigus, bullous pemphigoid, gestational pemphigoid, cicatricial pemphigoid, epidermolysis bullosa acquisita, linear IgA bullous dermatosis (LABD), dermatitis herpetiformis
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · 1. Primary cutaneous disorders (part 3) | VESICLES/BULLAE

items.title: Identifying Drug-Induced Bullous Diseases
items.content: Drug-induced bullous diseases, such as phototoxic eruptions and toxic epidermal necrolysis (TEN), present with distinct clinical features. Phototoxic eruptions resemble an exaggerated sunburn with diffuse erythema and bullae in sun-exposed areas, often linked to drugs like doxycycline and NSAIDs. TEN is characterized by widespread tender erythema and sloughing of skin, leading to significant morbidity and mortality.
keyPoints: Phototoxic eruptions are associated with drugs such as doxycycline, quinolones, voriconazole, thiazides, NSAIDs, vemurafenib, and psoralens.
TEN involves widespread tender erythema and sloughing of skin, with high morbidity and mortality rates.
Common drug offenders for TEN include aromatic anticonvulsants (phenytoin, barbiturates, carbamazepine), sulfonamides, aminopenicillins, allopurinol, and NSAIDs.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · TABLE 61-12 Causes of Vesicles/Bullae (part 1)

items.title: Distinguishing Erythema Multiforme from Other Conditions
items.content: Erythema multiforme (EM) is characterized by pink-red macules and edematous papules, which may become vesicular. The presence of target lesions or a 'dusky' violet center aids in diagnosis. EM minor is often due to HSV, while EM major can be associated with Mycoplasma pneumoniae and other pathogens.
keyPoints: EM primary lesions are pink-red macules and edematous papules that may become vesicular.
Target lesions or a 'dusky' violet center are diagnostic clues for EM.
EM minor is typically due to HSV, while EM major can be associated with Mycoplasma pneumoniae.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · TABLE 61-12 Causes of Vesicles/Bullae (part 1)

items.title: Differentiating Staphylococcal Scalded-Skin Syndrome from TEN
items.content: Staphylococcal scalded-skin syndrome (SSSS) and toxic epidermal necrolysis (TEN) can present similarly but have distinct features. SSSS primarily affects infants and toddlers, with superficial blister formation and no oral lesions, while TEN involves more extensive skin involvement and higher morbidity.
keyPoints: SSSS is characterized by redness and tenderness in central face, neck, trunk, and intertriginous zones, followed by flaccid bullae and exfoliation of the superficial epidermis.
TEN involves widespread tender erythema with significant skin sloughing and high morbidity and mortality rates.
SSSS is associated with staphylococcal exfoliative toxin, not drugs, distinguishing it from TEN.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · TABLE 61-12 Causes of Vesicles/Bullae (part 1)

items.title: Diagnosis of Porphyria Cutanea Tarda
items.content: Porphyria cutanea tarda (PCT) is a metabolic disorder characterized by blister formation in sun-exposed areas, particularly on the hands. The skin becomes fragile and trauma leads to erosions mixed with tense vesicles that heal with scarring and milia formation.
keyPoints: Blister formation occurs due to decreased cutaneous blood flow or metabolic disorders like PCT.
Lesions are located in sun-exposed areas, primarily the hands, and can be as large as 6 cm in diameter.
Characteristic findings include hypertrichosis of the lateral malar region (men) or face (women), hyperpigmentation, firm sclerotic plaques, and milia (firm, 1- to 2-mm white or yellow papules).
Diagnosis is confirmed by elevated urinary uroporphyrins due to decreased uroporphyrinogen decarboxylase activity.
PCT can be exacerbated by alcohol, hemochromatosis, chlorinated hydrocarbons, hepatitis C virus and HIV infections, and hepatomas.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · TABLE 61-12 Causes of Vesicles/Bullae (part 2)

items.title: Differential Diagnosis for PCT
items.content: The differential diagnosis of porphyria cutanea tarda includes several conditions with similar clinical presentations, such as porphyria variegata, drug-induced pseudoporphyria, bullous dermatosis of hemodialysis, and epidermolysis bullosa acquisita.
keyPoints: Porphyria variegata presents with skin signs of PCT plus systemic findings of acute intermittent porphyria; diagnostic plasma porphyrin fluorescence emission at 626 nm.
Drug-induced pseudoporphyria has clinical and histologic findings similar to PCT, but normal porphyrins; etiologic agents include naproxen, other NSAIDs, furosemide, tetracycline, and voriconazole.
Bullous dermatosis of hemodialysis appears like PCT, with usually normal or occasionally borderline elevated porphyrins in patients with chronic renal failure on hemodialysis.
PCT associated with hepatomas and hemodialysis presents similarly to classic PCT but occurs in specific patient populations.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · TABLE 61-12 Causes of Vesicles/Bullae (part 2)

items.title: Identifying Drug Reactions vs. Viral Infections in Morbilliform Eruptions
items.content: Morbilliform eruptions, characterized by erythematous macules and papules, are commonly due to drugs or viral infections. Up to 5% of patients receiving penicillins, sulfonamides, phenytoin, or nevirapine may develop these eruptions. It's crucial to differentiate between drug-induced rashes and those caused by underlying viral infections.
keyPoints: Morbilliform eruptions: erythematous macules and papules
Common causes: drugs (penicillins, sulfonamides, phenytoin, nevirapine) or viral infections
~5% incidence in patients on certain antibiotics
Accompanying signs may include pruritus, fever, eosinophilia, transaminitis, and transient lymphadenopathy
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · EXANTHEMS | TABLE 61-13 Causes of Exanthems (part 1)

items.title: Recognizing Atypical Measles in Adults
items.content: Atypical measles occurs in adults immunized with killed vaccines, presenting differently from classic measles. The eruption begins on the palms, soles, wrists, and ankles, potentially becoming purpuric. Pulmonary involvement is common, making it more severe than typical cases.
keyPoints: Occurs in unvaccinated or improperly vaccinated adults
Eruption starts on palms, soles, wrists, and ankles
Lesions may become purpuric
Pulmonary involvement can occur
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · EXANTHEMS | TABLE 61-13 Causes of Exanthems (part 1)

items.title: Distinguishing Scarlet Fever from Toxic Shock Syndrome and Kawasaki Disease
items.content: Scarlet fever, caused by erythrogenic toxin-producing group A streptococci, presents with diffuse erythema starting on the neck and upper trunk, red strawberry tongue, and desquamation of palms and soles 5–20 days after onset. Toxic shock syndrome (TSS) shares some features but involves mucous membrane hyperemia and is linked to staphylococcal exotoxins. Kawasaki disease can mimic these conditions but includes cervical lymphadenopathy, cheilitis, and thrombocytosis.
keyPoints: Scarlet fever: erythrogenic toxin from group A streptococci
Eruption starts on neck/upper trunk; desquamation 5–20 days later
TSS involves mucous membrane hyperemia, linked to staphylococcal exotoxins
Kawasaki disease: cervical lymphadenopathy, cheilitis, thrombocytosis
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · EXANTHEMS | TABLE 61-13 Causes of Exanthems (part 1)

items.title: Understanding the Etiologies and Clinical Features of Urticaria
items.content: Urticaria, commonly known as hives, presents as transient lesions characterized by a central wheal surrounded by an erythematous halo. These lesions can be round, oval, or figurate and are often pruritic. They may arise from various etiologies including allergic reactions, mastocytosis, thyroid disorders, Schnitzler's syndrome, and systemic-onset juvenile idiopathic arthritis (Still's disease). In Still's disease, urticarial lesions coincide with fever spikes due to neutrophilic infiltration.
keyPoints: Urticaria presents as transient wheals with an erythematous halo.
~5% of the population is affected by dermographism, a type of physical urticaria.
Solar urticaria occurs within minutes of sun exposure and can lead to pitted scarring on the nose and hands.
Cold urticaria may be associated with cryoglobulins or cryofibrinogens, leading to systemic symptoms like wheezing and syncope.
Cholinergic urticaria is triggered by heat, exercise, or emotion, presenting as small wheals with large flares.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · TABLE 61-13 Causes of Exanthems (part 2) | URTICARIA

items.title: Distinguishing Between Dermal and Subcutaneous Edema in Skin Lesions
items.content: Urticaria results from dermal edema, manifesting as wheals with erythematous halos. In contrast, angioedema involves subcutaneous edema affecting areas such as the eyelids, lips, tongue, larynx, and gastrointestinal tract. Angioedema can occur independently or alongside urticaria, including forms like urticarial vasculitis.
keyPoints: Urticaria is due to dermal edema, while angioedema results from subcutaneous edema.
Angioedema can affect the eyelids, lips, tongue, larynx, and gastrointestinal tract.
Both acquired and hereditary (autosomal dominant) forms of angioedema exist.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · TABLE 61-13 Causes of Exanthems (part 2) | URTICARIA

items.subtopic: TABLE 61-14 Causes of Urticaria and Angioedema | PAPULONODULAR SKIN LESIONS | WHITE LESIONS
items.title: Distinguishing Features of Urticarial Vasculitis from Simple Urticaria
items.content: Urticarial vasculitis is an immune complex disease that can be confused with simple urticaria. Unlike simple urticaria, lesions in urticarial vasculitis last longer than 24 hours and often develop central petechiae. Patients may experience burning rather than pruritus. Biopsy reveals leukocytoclastic vasculitis of small dermal blood vessels.
keyPoints: Lesions last longer than 24 hours
Central petechiae present after urticarial phase resolves
Burning sensation instead of pruritus
Leukocytoclastic vasculitis on biopsy
Hypocomplementemia in one- to two-thirds of patients, even idiopathic cases
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · TABLE 61-14 Causes of Urticaria and Angioedema | PAPULONODULAR SKIN LESIONS | WHITE LESIONS

items.subtopic: TABLE 61-14 Causes of Urticaria and Angioedema | PAPULONODULAR SKIN LESIONS | WHITE LESIONS
items.title: Characteristics and Associations of Calcinosis Cutis
items.content: Calcinosis cutis presents as firm white to white-yellow papules with an irregular surface. Dystrophic calcification occurs at sites of previous inflammation or skin damage, such as acne scars or systemic sclerosis. Metastatic calcinosis cutis involves nodules due to elevated calcium phosphate products and can lead to calciphylaxis.
keyPoints: Firm white to white-yellow papules with irregular surface
Dystrophic calcification at sites of previous inflammation or damage
Occurs in acne scars, systemic sclerosis, DM
Metastatic calcinosis cutis due to elevated calcium phosphate products
Can lead to calciphylaxis
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · TABLE 61-14 Causes of Urticaria and Angioedema | PAPULONODULAR SKIN LESIONS | WHITE LESIONS

items.title: Recognizing Rheumatoid Nodules in Clinical Practice
items.content: Rheumatoid nodules are firm subcutaneous nodules that commonly appear on the extensor aspects of joints, particularly elbows. They are associated with rheumatoid arthritis and can be indicative of underlying systemic disease.
keyPoints: Firm 0.5- to 4-cm nodules
~20% prevalence in patients with rheumatoid arthritis
~5% prevalence in patients with Still's disease
Biopsies show palisading granulomas
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · SKIN-COLORED LESIONS | 1. White

items.title: Identifying Neurofibromas and Associated Syndromes
items.content: Neurofibromas are soft papules or nodules that can be indicative of von Recklinghausen's disease (NF type I) when multiple lesions are present, along with other specific signs.
keyPoints: Soft papules or nodules with the 'button-hole' sign
Multiple neurofibromas suggest NF1
Associated features: six or more CALMs >1.5 cm, axillary freckling, multiple Lisch nodules
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · SKIN-COLORED LESIONS | 1. White

items.title: When to Evaluate for Tuberous Sclerosis or MEN Syndrome Type 1 in Patients with Multiple Angiofibromas
items.content: Angiofibromas are firm pink-red to skin-colored papules ranging from 3 mm to 1.5 cm in diameter. When multiple lesions are located on the central cheeks (adenoma sebaceum), it is crucial to evaluate for tuberous sclerosis or MEN syndrome, type 1.
keyPoints: Angiofibromas measure 3 mm to 1.5 cm
Multiple lesions on central cheeks suggest adenoma sebaceum
Evaluate for tuberous sclerosis or MEN syndrome, type 1
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · IV. Yellow | V. Red $ ^{b} $

items.title: Investigating MEN Syndrome Type 2b in Patients with Multiple Neuromas
items.content: Neuromas are firm, skin-colored papules commonly found at sites of amputations and in rudimentary polydactyly. When multiple neuromas appear on the eyelids, lips, distal tongue, or oral mucosa, investigate for MEN syndrome type 2b.
keyPoints: Neuromas are firm, skin-colored papules
Multiple neuromas on specific sites suggest MEN syndrome type 2b
Associated findings include marfanoid habitus and medullary thyroid carcinoma (>75% of patients)
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · IV. Yellow | V. Red $ ^{b} $

items.title: Considering Basal Cell Nevus Syndrome in Patients with Multiple BCCs Prior to Age 30
items.content: Basal cell carcinomas (BCCs) are translucent papules with rolled borders, telangiectasias, and central erosion. When multiple BCCs occur, especially before age 30, consider basal cell nevus syndrome.
keyPoints: BCCs have rolled borders, telangiectasias, and central erosion
Multiple BCCs prior to age 30 suggest basal cell nevus syndrome
Associated findings include jaw cysts, palmar and plantar pits, frontal bossing
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · IV. Yellow | V. Red $ ^{b} $

items.title: Diagnosis and Differentiation of Pink Lesions in Primary Systemic Amyloidosis
items.content: Pink to pink-orange lesions, particularly in the periorbital and perioral regions, are indicative of primary systemic amyloidosis. These lesions result from increased vessel wall fragility due to amyloid deposits, leading to pinch purpura. Diagnosis can be confirmed through histologic examination of abdominal subcutaneous fat and serum free light chain assay.
keyPoints: Lesions often pink to pink-orange in color
Common locations: face (periorbital/perioral), flexural areas
Histologic exam of abdominal subcutaneous fat confirms diagnosis
~30% of patients with primary systemic amyloidosis have specific mucocutaneous lesions
Pinch purpura due to increased vessel wall fragility from amyloid deposits
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · PINK LESIONS

items.title: Differentiating Multicentric Reticulohistiocytosis and Papular Mucinosis
items.content: Multicentric reticulohistiocytosis presents with pink papules and nodules, often mimicking rheumatoid arthritis due to associated polyarthritis. Histologic examination reveals giant cells not seen in rheumatoid nodules. Papular mucinosis features firm, skin-colored papules (2–5 mm) in a linear arrangement, with biopsy showing localized mucin deposition and serum protein electrophoresis indicating a monoclonal spike of IgG.
keyPoints: Multicentric reticulohistiocytosis: pink papules/nodules on face, hands, forearms; polyarthritis
Histologic giant cells in multicentric reticulohistiocytosis not seen in rheumatoid nodules
Papular mucinosis: firm, skin-colored papules (2–5 mm), linear arrangement
Biopsy of papular mucinosis shows localized mucin deposition
Monoclonal spike of IgG with $ \lambda $ light chain in serum protein electrophoresis
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · PINK LESIONS

items.title: Identifying Yellow Lesions and Their Associations with Systemic Disorders
items.content: Yellow lesions on the skin can indicate several systemic disorders. Eruptive xanthomas, associated with hypertriglyceridemia, are common in uncontrolled diabetes mellitus. Tophaceous gout presents as firm yellow to yellow-white deposits around joints, while necrobiosis lipoidica is characterized by central yellow plaques primarily on the shins. Pseudoxanthoma elasticum involves calcium deposition leading to reticulated plaques.
keyPoints: Eruptive xanthomas are associated with hyperlipoproteinemia types I, IV, and V and often resolve with a fall in serum triglycerides.
Tophaceous gout deposits can be identified by polarized light microscopy of aspirated contents.
Necrobiosis lipoidica is found primarily on the shins (90%) and may develop or indicate diabetes mellitus.
Pseudoxanthoma elasticum involves calcium deposition on elastic fibers, leading to reticulated plaques.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · YELLOW LESIONS

items.title: Differential Diagnosis of Yellow Skin Lesions
items.content: Yellow skin lesions can be differentiated by their location and associated systemic conditions. Xanthelasma occurs on the eyelids and is not specific for hyperlipidemia, as 50% of patients with normal lipid profiles have this lesion. Tendon xanthomas are found on tendons like the Achilles and extensor finger tendons. Plane xanthomas can be normolipemic in plasma cell dyscrasias and may exceed 12 cm in diameter.
keyPoints: Xanthelasma, found on eyelids, is not specific for hyperlipidemia; at least 50% of patients with normal lipid profiles have this lesion.
Tendon xanthomas are associated with Achilles and extensor finger tendons.
Normolipemic plane xanthomas in plasma cell dyscrasias can be ≥12 cm in diameter.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · YELLOW LESIONS

items.title: Clinical Implications of Yellow Lesions in Systemic Disorders
items.content: Yellow lesions such as xanthomas and tophi have significant clinical implications. Xanthomas indicate hyperlipidemia or hypertriglyceridemia, with eruptive xanthomas commonly linked to uncontrolled diabetes mellitus. Tophaceous gout requires identification via polarized light microscopy for diagnosis. Necrobiosis lipoidica is often associated with diabetes mellitus and can lead to ulcerations.
keyPoints: Eruptive xanthomas are most common in uncontrolled diabetes mellitus.
Tophaceous gout deposits are firm, yellow to yellow-white, and can discharge chalky material.
Necrobiosis lipoidica lesions on the shins may develop into ulcers.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · YELLOW LESIONS

items.title: Identifying Bacillary Angiomatosis in AIDS Patients
items.content: In patients with AIDS, multiple red hemangioma-like lesions may indicate bacillary angiomatosis. This condition is caused by Bartonella henselae and Bartonella quintana, identifiable via biopsy stained with Warthin-Starry.
keyPoints: Multiple red hemangioma-like lesions in AIDS suggest bacillary angiomatosis.
Biopsy shows clusters of bacilli staining positively with Warthin-Starry stain.
Pathogens identified are Bartonella henselae and Bartonella quintana.
Disseminated visceral disease can occur primarily in immunocompromised hosts.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · RED LESIONS (part 1)

items.title: Diagnosing Fabry Disease with Angiokeratomas
items.content: Multiple angiokeratomas, red to red-purple papules measuring 1–3 mm, are indicative of Fabry disease. Diagnosis is confirmed through genetic analysis for alpha-galactosidase A deficiency.
keyPoints: Angiokeratomas in Fabry disease appear as small (1-3 mm) red to red-purple lesions.
Commonly located on the lower trunk.
Associated with chronic renal disease, peripheral neuropathy, and corneal opacities.
Diagnosis is confirmed by genetic analysis for alpha-galactosidase A deficiency.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · RED LESIONS (part 1)

items.title: Differentiating PMLE from Cutaneous Lupus
items.content: Polymorphous light eruption (PMLE) presents as erythematous papules and plaques in sun-exposed areas, with a seasonal pattern. It must be differentiated from cutaneous lupus through history, histology, and direct immunofluorescence.
keyPoints: PMLE causes erythematous papules/plaques on sun-exposed skin (e.g., dorsum of hand).
Occurs primarily in late spring/early summer; fades with UV exposure but recurs seasonally.
Differentiation from cutaneous lupus involves observing natural history and histologic examination.
Direct immunofluorescence may be used for differentiation.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · RED LESIONS (part 1)

items.title: Lymphoma Cutis: Clinical Presentation and Associations
items.content: Lymphoma cutis involves clonal proliferation of malignant lymphocytes in the skin, presenting as infiltrated pink-red to red-purple papules and plaques. It can occur anywhere on the skin but often affects areas like the malar ridge, tip of the nose, and earlobes. Non-Hodgkin's lymphomas are more likely to present with specific cutaneous lesions compared to Hodgkin's lymphoma.
keyPoints: Lymphoma cutis resembles cutaneous lymphoid hyperplasia.
Common sites: malar ridge, tip of the nose, earlobes.
Non-Hodgkin's lymphomas more likely than Hodgkin's to have specific cutaneous lesions.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · RED LESIONS (part 2)

items.title: Sweet Syndrome: Clinical Features and Associations
items.content: Sweet syndrome is characterized by painful pink-red to red-brown edematous plaques, primarily on the head, neck, and upper extremities. It often presents with fever, neutrophilia, and a dense dermal infiltrate of neutrophils. Approximately 10% of patients have an associated malignancy, most commonly acute myelogenous leukemia.
keyPoints: Painful pink-red to red-brown edematous plaques.
Common sites: head, neck, upper extremities.
~10% of cases are associated with malignancies like acute myelogenous leukemia.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · RED LESIONS (part 2)

items.title: Erythema Nodosum: Clinical Presentation and Associations
items.content: Erythema nodosum presents as subcutaneous nodules, initially red but turning blue as they resolve. It is commonly associated with streptococcal infections, upper respiratory viral infections, sarcoidosis, and inflammatory bowel disease. The most common location for these nodules is the skin.
keyPoints: Subcutaneous nodules that turn from red to blue.
Common associations: streptococcal infections, sarcoidosis, IBD.
Most common site: skin.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · RED LESIONS (part 2)

items.title: Diagnosis and Management of Cutaneous Polyarteritis Nodosa
items.content: Cutaneous polyarteritis nodosa, also known as cutaneous arteritis, presents with painful subcutaneous nodules and ulcers within a red-purple, netlike pattern of livedo reticularis. These lesions are primarily located on the lower extremities and may be accompanied by arthralgias and myalgias, but without systemic involvement. A skin biopsy will show necrotizing vasculitis or granulomatous inflammation.
keyPoints: Painful subcutaneous nodules and ulcers in a red-purple pattern of livedo reticularis
Lesions primarily on lower extremities
No systemic involvement despite possible arthralgias and myalgias
Biopsy shows necrotizing vasculitis or granulomatous inflammation
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · RED LESIONS (part 3) | RED-BROWN LESIONS

items.title: Differential Diagnosis of Sarcoidosis with Red to Red-Brown Lesions
items.content: Sarcoidosis presents with red to red-brown cutaneous lesions that show a yellow-brown color on diascopy due to granulomatous infiltrate. Common locations include the face, and biopsies reveal 'naked' granulomas in the dermis. Differential diagnoses include foreign-body granulomas, late secondary syphilis, reactive granulomatous dermatitis, and lupus vulgaris.
keyPoints: Red to red-brown lesions with yellow-brown color on diascopy
Commonly found on the face
Biopsy shows 'naked' granulomas in dermis
Differential diagnoses: foreign-body granulomas, late secondary syphilis, reactive granulomatous dermatitis, lupus vulgaris
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · RED LESIONS (part 3) | RED-BROWN LESIONS

items.subtopic: BLUE LESIONS | VIOLACEOUS LESIONS | PURPLE LESIONS
items.title: Blue Lesions: Identify Underlying Causes and Syndromes
items.content: Blue lesions in the skin can result from vascular ectasias, hyperplasias, tumors, or melanin pigment within the dermis. Key conditions include venous lakes, venous malformations, blue rubber bleb syndrome, Maffucci's syndrome, and blue nevi. Blue rubber bleb syndrome involves gastrointestinal bleeding due to vascular anomalies, while Maffucci's syndrome includes associated osteochondromas.
keyPoints: Blue lesions result from vascular ectasias, hyperplasias, tumors, or melanin pigment in the dermis.
Venous lakes are compressible dark-blue lesions commonly found on the head and neck.
Multiple papulonodules suggest blue rubber bleb syndrome (mutations in TEK) with gastrointestinal bleeding risk.
Maffucci's syndrome involves multiple nodules (mutations in IDH1) with associated osteochondromas.
Blue nevi are benign dome-shaped lesions due to pigment-producing cells, common on hands/feet and head/neck.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · BLUE LESIONS | VIOLACEOUS LESIONS | PURPLE LESIONS

items.subtopic: BLUE LESIONS | VIOLACEOUS LESIONS | PURPLE LESIONS
items.title: Purple Lesions: Distinguish Between Vascular Tumors and Other Conditions
items.content: Purple lesions can indicate vascular tumors like Kaposi's sarcoma or angiosarcoma, or conditions such as palpable purpura due to red blood cell extravasation. Pseudo-Kaposi's sarcoma (acral angiodermatitis) may mimic these tumors in appearance and histology but results from venous hypertension.
keyPoints: Purple lesions can be caused by vascular tumors or inflammation leading to red blood cell extravasation.
Kaposi's sarcoma presents as purple papules/plaques, often on the lower extremities.
Angiosarcoma commonly appears on the scalp and face of elderly patients or in chronic lymphedema areas.
Pseudo-Kaposi's sarcoma (acral angiodermatitis) resembles Kaposi's sarcoma but is due to venous hypertension.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · BLUE LESIONS | VIOLACEOUS LESIONS | PURPLE LESIONS

items.subtopic: BROWN AND BLACK LESIONS | CUTANEOUS METASTASES | PURPURA
items.title: Recognition and Implications of Cutaneous Metastases
items.content: Cutaneous metastases can present as firm, skin-colored subcutaneous nodules or red to red-brown papulonodules. They may also appear pink, blue, or black if due to metastatic melanoma. These lesions are significant because they often indicate hematogenous or lymphatic spread from primary carcinomas such as melanoma (in both men and women), lung, colon (men), breast, and ovary (women). Notably, cutaneous metastases might be the first sign of carcinoma, particularly with lung primaries.
keyPoints: Cutaneous metastases can present as firm nodules or papulonodules in various colors including skin-colored, red to red-brown, pink, blue, or black.
Common primary carcinomas leading to cutaneous metastases: melanoma (men and women), lung (men), colon (men), breast (women), ovary (women).
Cutaneous metastases may be the initial presentation of carcinoma, especially with lung primaries.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · BROWN AND BLACK LESIONS | CUTANEOUS METASTASES | PURPURA

items.subtopic: BROWN AND BLACK LESIONS | CUTANEOUS METASTASES | PURPURA
items.title: Understanding Purpura and Its Clinical Significance
items.content: Purpura is characterized by the extravasation of red blood cells into the dermis, resulting in non-blanching lesions that appear erythematous or violet-colored. This condition contrasts with other skin discolorations as purpuric lesions do not disappear under pressure.
keyPoints: Purpura results from the extravasation of red blood cells into the dermis.
Lesions are non-blanching and appear erythematous or violet-colored.
Distinguishing feature: Purpuric lesions do not blanch with pressure.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · BROWN AND BLACK LESIONS | CUTANEOUS METASTASES | PURPURA

items.title: Nonpalpable Purpura: Primary Cutaneous Disorders
items.content: Primary cutaneous disorders such as trauma, solar purpura, stasis purpura, and capillaritis are the most frequent causes of nonpalpable purpura. These conditions result from alterations in connective tissue or perivascular inflammation.
keyPoints: Nonpalpable purpura includes primary cutaneous disorders like trauma and solar purpura.
Solar purpura primarily affects extensor forearms, while steroid purpura can be more widespread.
Capillaritis results in bright red petechiae (1–2 mm) within yellow-brown patches on the lower extremities due to perivascular lymphocytic inflammation.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · TABLE 61-16 Causes of Purpura (part 1)

items.title: Systemic Causes: Clotting Disturbances and Vascular Fragility
items.content: Nonpalpable purpura can result from systemic causes such as clotting disturbances, including thrombocytopenia, abnormal platelet function, and clotting factor defects. Vascular fragility due to conditions like amyloidosis, Ehlers-Danlos syndrome, and scurvy also leads to nonpalpable purpura.
keyPoints: Thrombocytopenia-induced petechiae often present initially on the distal lower extremity.
Vascular fragility causes nonpalpable purpura in systemic amyloidosis, Ehlers-Danlos syndrome, and scurvy.
Scurvy presents with flattened corkscrew hairs and perifollicular hemorrhage due to vitamin C deficiency affecting collagen cross-link formation.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · TABLE 61-16 Causes of Purpura (part 1)

items.title: Noninflammatory Purpura: Thrombi Formation
items.content: Noninflammatory purpura associated with thrombi formation can be seen in conditions like disseminated intravascular coagulation, monoclonal cryoglobulinemia, and antiphospholipid antibody syndrome. These conditions often present with retiform purpura and are confirmed by skin biopsy.
keyPoints: Disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC) can cause widespread purpura and hemorrhagic infarcts, triggered by infections or tissue injury.
Monoclonal cryoglobulinemia is linked to plasma cell dyscrasias and presents with purpura on the lower extremities.
Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura involves microangiopathic hemolytic anemia and neurologic abnormalities.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · TABLE 61-16 Causes of Purpura (part 1)

items.title: Warfarin-induced necrosis: Recognizing risk factors and timing
items.content: Warfarin can cause painful erythema that progresses to purpuric lesions and then necrotic black eschar, known as Coumadin®-induced necrosis. This typically occurs between the third and tenth day of therapy due to an imbalance in vitamin K-dependent factors.
keyPoints: More common in women and areas with abundant subcutaneous fat (breasts, abdomen, buttocks, thighs, calves).
Erythema and purpura develop between days 3-10 of warfarin therapy.
Increased risk in patients with protein C deficiency.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · TABLE 61-16 Causes of Purpura (part 2)

items.title: Cholesterol emboli: Identifying clinical signs and biopsy findings
items.content: Purpura from cholesterol emboli typically affects the lower extremities in patients with atherosclerotic disease, often following anticoagulant therapy or vascular procedures. Key features include livedo reticularis, gangrene, cyanosis, ischemic ulcerations, and peripheral eosinophilia.
keyPoints: Occurs on lower extremities post-vascular procedures or spontaneously from plaque disintegration.
Associated with livedo reticularis, gangrene, cyanosis, ischemic ulcerations, and peripheral eosinophilia.
Biopsy may require multiple sections to show cholesterol clefts.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · TABLE 61-16 Causes of Purpura (part 2)

items.title: Vasculitic palpable purpura: Understanding etiologies and systemic associations
items.content: Palpable purpura in vasculitis, particularly leukocytoclastic vasculitis (LCV), is linked to drugs, infections, and autoimmune diseases. Henoch-Schönlein purpura (HSP) is a subtype seen post-respiratory infection, with systemic symptoms like fever and nephritis.
keyPoints: Commonly associated with drugs, infections, and autoimmune diseases.
Henoch-Schönlein purpura often follows upper respiratory infections in children.
Systemic manifestations include fever, arthralgias, abdominal pain, gastrointestinal bleeding, and nephritis.
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · TABLE 61-16 Causes of Purpura (part 2)

items.title: Diagnosis and Management of Ecthyma Gangrenosum
items.content: Ecthyma gangrenosum is characterized by lesions that begin as edematous, erythematous papules or plaques which develop central purpura and necrosis. These lesions frequently occur in the girdle region and are associated with Pseudomonas aeruginosa infections but can also be caused by other gram-negative rods such as Klebsiella, Escherichia coli, and Serratia. In immunocompromised hosts, opportunistic fungi like Candida, Aspergillus, and Fusarium should also be considered.
keyPoints: Lesions start as edematous, erythematous papules or plaques
Develop central purpura and necrosis with bullae formation
Classically associated with Pseudomonas aeruginosa
Other gram-negative rods (Klebsiella, E. coli, Serratia) can cause similar lesions
In immunocompromised hosts, consider Candida, Aspergillus, Fusarium
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · TABLE 61-16 Causes of Purpura (part 3) | ULCERS

items.title: Clinical Features and Diagnosis of Rocky Mountain Spotted Fever
items.content: Rocky Mountain spotted fever is a tick-borne disease caused by Rickettsia rickettsii. It presents with a history of fever, chills, severe headache, and photophobia preceding the onset of cutaneous eruption. Initial lesions are erythematous macules and papules on wrists, ankles, palms, and soles, which spread centripetally to become purpuric.
keyPoints: Caused by Rickettsia rickettsii
Preceded by fever, chills, severe headache, photophobia
Initial lesions: erythematous macules/papules on wrists, ankles, palms, soles
Lesions spread centripetally and become purpuric
items.reference: Eczema Psoriasis Cutaneous Infections Acne and Other Common Skin Disor · TABLE 61-16 Causes of Purpura (part 3) | ULCERS

items.title: Understanding the Etiology of Peripheral Vascular Disease in Mucocutaneous Ulcers
items.content: Peripheral vascular disease, including venous and arterial causes, is a primary cutaneous disorder leading to mucocutaneous ulcers. Recognizing these etiologies helps guide appropriate diagnostic evaluations and management strategies.
keyPoints: Venous and arterial issues are primary causes of peripheral vascular disease-related ulcers.
Arterial involvement in mucocutaneous ulcers is noted with a reference to further details (indicated by $^{a}$).
Bedside pearl: Assess for signs of venous insufficiency or arterial occlusion when evaluating lower extremity ulcers.

items.title: Identifying Systemic Diseases Causing Lower Leg Ulcers
items.content: Systemic diseases such as vasculitis, hemoglobinopathies, and cryoglobulinemia can manifest with mucocutaneous ulcers on the lower legs. Awareness of these conditions is crucial for comprehensive patient evaluation.
keyPoints: Small-vessel and medium-vessel vasculitis are implicated in lower leg ulcers (indicated by $^{c}$).
Cryoglobulinemia and cryofibrinogenemia are systemic diseases associated with lower leg ulcers, referenced by $^{c}$.
Cholesterol emboli can cause lower leg ulcers, as noted with references $^{a,c}$.
Bedside pearl: Consider systemic causes when lower extremity ulcers do not respond to standard wound care.
